draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Runners want real-time feedback on their current speed through animated visual rhythm, not static numbers
facade.label: Pace indicator pulses
facade.acceptImplies: Design tempo and animation rate as core navigation — heartbeat, breathing, stride cadence become visual metaphors
facade.rejectImplies: Prioritize clarity and glanceability over motion — use static gauges, fixed numbers, stable layouts

evidence.hypothesis: Runners want real-time feedback on their current speed through animated visual rhythm, not static numbers
evidence.implication: Design tempo and animation rate as core navigation — heartbeat, breathing, stride cadence become visual metaphors

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners works best when it feels like a friend giving quick tips, not a formal weather service
facade.label: Casual chat tone
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into conversational language, encouraging messages, maybe even humor or personality in notifications and UI copy
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward clean, neutral, minimal copy—let data and icons do the talking without personality overlay

facade.hypothesis: A clean, high-contrast interface with lots of white space will feel energetic and focused—appropriate for runners in action who need quick, glanceable info.
facade.axisTargeted: Warm vs cool + Calm vs energetic
facade.label: Bright and minimal
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers digital clarity over organic warmth; values speed and scan-ability; may want neon accents or bold typography.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants a more grounded, tactile aesthetic; prefers softer contrast and breathing room; may favor warm tones or illustration-based design.

facade.hypothesis: Runners need weather data AND navigation together, not sequentially
facade.axisTargeted: Information density vs cognitive load — showing multiple data layers simultaneously vs showing one focus at a time
facade.label: Split screen: conditions + route
facade.acceptImplies: Users value integrated context; design should layer weather into navigation rather than keep them separate; dense-but-scannable layouts work for runners in motion
facade.rejectImplies: Runners prefer focused, single-task screens; weather data should be modal/overlay or separate tab; information should be sequential, not simultaneous

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer low visual complexity with high contrast for quick glance readability during movement
facade.label: Minimal dark cards
facade.acceptImplies: Design should favor stripped-down card layouts with dark backgrounds and bright text/data for rapid information parsing
facade.rejectImplies: Design could accommodate more visual detail, colorful gradients, or lighter backgrounds without sacrificing usability

facade.hypothesis: A left sidebar holds persistent info (weather alerts, route history) while top tabs switch between views (current, hourly, map). This hybrid gives runners quick glance data without losing context.
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure: sidebar persistence vs tab-based switching
facade.label: Sidebar + tabs combo
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers stable left-panel info anchoring over pure tab switching. Informs preference for always-visible runner data.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers cleaner, simpler navigation—either full sidebar OR tabs, not hybrid. Opens door to pure-tab or bottom-nav patterns.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented vibe
Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, immediate feedback

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented vibe
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, immediate feedback
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners perceive weather shifts (sun to cloud, warm to cold) as gradual transitions rather than sudden changes, and smooth morphing animations feel more trustworthy than snap-cuts when displaying condition updates
facade.axisTargeted: Fluid vs snappy — do weather state changes animate smoothly or pop instantly?
facade.label: Transitions blur between states
facade.acceptImplies: Animation duration and easing curves become core UX decisions; weather app should mirror how runners *feel* environmental shifts in their body, not how data timestamps them
facade.rejectImplies: Runners prioritize instant clarity over motion continuity; animation overhead is friction; state changes should be atomic and immediate for quick glance-ability

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
